column3: General_characteristics_Australia_focused
column6: Familiar / interesting native Australian examples and approximate diversity

column3: Poaceae, the true grass family, is Australia’s third-largest flowering plant family, with approximately 1,300 species recorded nationally. Grasses occur in almost every Australian habitat, from tropical savannas and alpine plains to deserts and coastal dunes. In many inland regions they dominate the landscape, particularly the hummock grasslands of arid Australia and the temperate grassy woodlands of the southeast. Ecologically, grasses are foundational species that influence fire regimes, erosion control, grazing systems and carbon storage. Economically, the family includes globally important cereal crops, while native grasses are increasingly valued in ecological restoration and low-water landscaping.
column6: Spinifex (Triodia spp.; ~65 species), kangaroo grass (Themeda triandra), wallaby grasses (Rytidosperma spp.; ~100 species), poa tussock grasses (Poa spp.). Spinifex grasslands cover enormous areas of the Australian outback and provide critical shelter for reptiles, birds and small mammals. Some native grasses, such as Micraira, are remarkable “resurrection plants” capable of surviving near-complete desiccation and reviving after rain. Indigenous Australians have long used native grasses for seed harvesting, weaving and habitat management through cultural burning.

column3: Fabaceae is one of Australia’s largest and most ecologically significant flowering plant families. Many species form symbiotic relationships with nitrogen-fixing bacteria, allowing them to thrive in nutrient-poor Australian soils. The family ranges from towering rainforest trees to desert shrubs and delicate pea flowers. Many species are highly adapted to fire and disturbance and flower prolifically after rain.
column6: Wattles (Acacia spp.; ~1,000 Australian species), Sturt’s desert pea (Swainsona formosa), blackwood (Acacia melanoxylon). Acacia is Australia’s largest genus of flowering plants and the golden wattle is the national floral emblem. Wattle flowering is culturally significant and widely associated with the beginning of spring in Australia.

column3: Myrtaceae is one of the defining plant families of Australia and dominates much of the continent’s forests and woodlands. Most species contain aromatic oils in their leaves and possess strong fire adaptations such as epicormic resprouting or lignotubers. Australia is a global centre of diversity for the family and many species are ecologically dominant canopy trees.
column5: Syncarpia glomulifera, Lophostemon confertus, Backhousia-type wet taxa
column6: Eucalypts (Eucalyptus, Corymbia, Angophora; ~900 species), paperbarks (Melaleuca spp.; ~300 species), bottlebrushes (Callistemon spp.), lilly pillies (Syzygium spp.). Old eucalypts develop hollows essential for parrots, possums, bats and owls, though these hollows can take more than a century to form. Eucalypt oils evolved partly as protection against heat stress and herbivory.

column3: Traditionally recognised as the saltbush family, this group contains many drought- and salt-tolerant species adapted to Australia’s inland and semi-arid regions. Many species have succulent or silvery-grey leaves that reduce water loss and reflect heat. Chenopod shrublands are among the defining vegetation types of inland Australia.
column6: Saltbushes (Atriplex spp.; ~90 Australian species), bluebushes (Maireana spp.; ~60 species), samphires (Tecticornia spp.; ~40 species). These plants can survive in highly saline soils where few others can grow and are important forage plants in rangelands.

column3: Asteraceae is one of the largest flowering plant families in Australia and globally. Members are characterised by composite flower heads made up of many tiny individual flowers packed together to resemble a single bloom. Australian native daisies are especially prominent in grasslands, alpine regions and arid landscapes.
column5: Mostly alpine/wet heath taxa rather than rainforest
column6: Everlasting daisies (Xerochrysum spp.), billy buttons (Pycnosorus spp.), native daisies (Brachyscome spp.; ~70 species). Inland Australia can experience spectacular mass flowering events after rain, transforming dry landscapes into colourful wildflower displays.

column3: Malvaceae includes shrubs and trees often recognised by soft leaves, fibrous bark and large showy flowers. Australian representatives range from rainforest trees to drought-adapted outback species. Many have cultural significance and are widely used in horticulture.
column6: Kurrajongs (Brachychiton spp.; ~30 species), native hibiscus (Hibiscus heterophyllus), boab (Adansonia gregorii). Boabs can store large amounts of water in swollen trunks, helping them survive long dry seasons in northern Australia. Kurrajong seeds were traditionally eaten after roasting.

column3: Sedges are grass-like plants especially common in wetlands, swamps and along waterways. Unlike grasses, they usually have solid triangular stems, leading to the field saying “sedges have edges”. Sedges play an important ecological role in water filtration and wetland habitat structure.
column5: Wet forest sedges, not rainforest canopy taxa
column6: Sword sedges (Lepidosperma spp.; ~70 species), spike-rushes (Eleocharis spp.), umbrella sedges (Cyperus spp.). Sedges provide important shelter and nesting habitat for frogs, fish and wetland birds and are increasingly used in urban water-sensitive design.

column3: Australia is one of the world’s great centres of diversity for the ancient Proteaceae family. Many species are highly adapted to nutrient-poor soils through specialised proteoid roots and to fire through woody seed cones or underground resprouting structures. The family is especially prominent in southwestern Australia and eastern heathlands.
column6: Banksias (Banksia spp.; ~170 species), grevilleas (Grevillea spp.; ~360 Australian species), hakeas (Hakea spp.; ~150 species), waratahs (Telopea spp.). Banksia flower spikes can contain thousands of tiny nectar-rich flowers that support birds, insects and mammals. Macadamia is Australia’s only globally commercialised native food crop.

column3: Scrophulariaceae includes mostly herbs and shrubs with tubular flowers adapted for insect pollination. Australian representatives often occur in semi-arid habitats and can appear rapidly after rainfall events. Historically, many genera once placed in this family have been reassigned following DNA studies.
column6: Stemodia spp., native foxglove relatives and various small annual herbs. Several species are short-lived opportunists that flourish after seasonal rains.

column3: Goodeniaceae is a distinctly Australian family best known for its unusual fan-shaped flowers and diverse heathland species. Many species occur in sandy soils, coastal dunes and nutrient-poor shrublands. Australia is the global centre of diversity for the family.
column6: Fan flowers (Scaevola spp.; ~130 species), Goodenia spp. (~180 species). The unusual split-petal flowers are highly distinctive and many species are important nectar plants for native insects.

column3: Sapindaceae includes rainforest trees, shrubs and climbers with colourful fleshy fruits often dispersed by birds. Many Australian species occur in tropical and subtropical rainforests.
column6: Native tamarinds (Diploglottis spp.), hairy pear fruits (Alectryon spp.; ~20 species). Several species produce edible fruits and play important roles in rainforest food webs.

column3: Euphorbiaceae is a diverse family of herbs, shrubs and trees often characterised by milky sap and specialised seed capsules. Many Australian species occur in rainforests and dry woodlands.
column6: Cheese trees (Glochidion ferdinandi), snowbush relatives (Breynia spp.). Some species disperse seeds explosively, ejecting them several metres from the parent plant.

column3: Convolvulaceae includes climbing vines and trailing herbs with trumpet-shaped flowers. Australian species occur in coastal systems, forests and inland habitats.
column6: Beach morning glory (Ipomoea pes-caprae), bindweeds (Convolvulus spp.). Coastal species play an important role in stabilising sand dunes against erosion.

column3: Rubiaceae includes rainforest shrubs and small trees often with glossy opposite leaves and fleshy fruits. Many species are bird dispersed and important components of subtropical rainforests.
column6: Native coffees (Psychotria spp.), native gardenias (Atractocarpus spp.). Fruits are eaten by rainforest birds and mammals, assisting seed dispersal through dense forests.

column3: Australian members of Asparagaceae include hardy monocots adapted to drought, fire and poor soils. Many species survive harsh conditions through underground rhizomes or storage organs.
column6: Mat-rushes (Lomandra spp.; ~50 species), scrambling lily (Geitonoplesium cymosum). Lomandra leaves were traditionally used for weaving baskets, nets and string.

column3: Amaranthaceae includes many hardy herbs and shrubs adapted to arid, saline and disturbed environments. The family is especially important in inland Australia.
column6: Joyweeds (Alternanthera spp.), pigface relatives and desert herbs. Many species tolerate extreme salinity, drought and heat that would kill less specialised plants.

column3: Ericaceae is especially important in Australian heathlands and alpine ecosystems where soils are nutrient poor and acidic. Many species produce nectar-rich flowers attractive to birds and insects.
column5: Epacris, Richea, Acrothamnus (cool temperate wet systems)
column6: Australian heaths (Epacris spp.; ~40 species), pink mountain berry (Leptecophylla juniperina). Heathlands are among Australia’s most species-rich vegetation communities.

column3: Santalaceae includes parasitic and semi-parasitic plants that connect to the roots of neighbouring species to obtain water and nutrients. The family is widespread in arid and semi-arid Australia.
column6: Quandongs (Santalum acuminatum), native cherries (Exocarpos cupressiformis). Quandong fruits were important traditional bushfoods and are still widely used in jams and desserts today.

column3: Lamiaceae contains aromatic herbs and shrubs usually recognised by square stems and paired leaves. Many Australian species contain essential oils and are important nectar plants.
column6: Native mints (Prostanthera spp.; ~100 species), coastal rosemary (Westringia fruticosa). Many species release strong fragrances when crushed and are popular garden plants.

column3: Lauraceae is a rainforest family of aromatic trees and shrubs producing fleshy fruits. Australian representatives are concentrated in subtropical and tropical rainforests.
column5: Lauraceae are classic rainforest trees though few/no obvious strong examples in this list
column6: Laurel trees (Cryptocarya spp.; ~20 Australian species), sassafras (Doryphora sassafras). Many fruits are eaten by cassowaries and rainforest pigeons which disperse seeds over long distances.

column3: Dilleniaceae includes shrubs and climbers often recognised by large yellow flowers and numerous prominent stamens. Australia is a major centre of diversity for the family.
column6: Guinea flowers (Hibbertia spp.; >150 Australian species). Hibbertia is one of Australia’s largest endemic flowering plant genera and is especially diverse in heathlands.

column3: Apocynaceae includes climbers, shrubs and trees often producing milky sap and elaborate flowers. Many Australian species occur in rainforests and tropical woodlands.
column6: Kangaroo vine (Marsdenia rostrata), native jasmines (Parsonsia spp.), wax flowers (Hoya spp.). Many species produce silky wind-dispersed seeds adapted for long-distance dispersal.

column3: Solanaceae includes herbs and shrubs with star-shaped flowers and fleshy berries. Australia has a remarkable diversity of native Solanum species adapted to arid and semi-arid environments.
column6: Kangaroo apples (Solanum spp.), native tobaccos (Nicotiana spp.). Several native Solanum species are pollinated by buzz-pollinating bees that vibrate flowers to release pollen.

column3: Casuarinaceae includes Australia’s distinctive she-oaks and she-banksias, recognised by needle-like branchlets that resemble pine needles. Many species are adapted to poor soils and dry climates.
column6: She-oaks (Casuarina spp.; 6 Australian species), Allocasuarina spp. (~59 species). Their “needles” are actually modified stems and many species fix atmospheric nitrogen through root symbioses.

column3: Rutaceae includes aromatic shrubs and trees rich in essential oils. Australia is especially diverse in native boronias and rainforest citrus relatives.
column6: Boronias (Boronia spp.; ~160 species), corkwoods (Melicope spp.), pink euodia (Melicope elleryana). Boronias are prized for their fragrance and are among Australia’s most admired native flowering shrubs.

column3: Aizoaceae is best known in Australia for succulent coastal and arid-zone plants adapted to drought, salt spray and intense sunlight. Many species have fleshy water-storing leaves and brightly coloured flowers that open in strong sunlight.
column6: Pigfaces (Carpobrotus spp.; ~6 Australian species), noon flowers (Disphyma spp.). Pigface fruits were eaten by Indigenous Australians and the plants are important dune stabilisers along Australian coastlines.

column3: Brassicaceae includes herbs with four-petalled flowers arranged in a cross shape, giving rise to the older family name Cruciferae. Australian native species occur in alpine, arid and coastal habitats, though the family is also well known globally for vegetables and agricultural crops. Many Australian species are small annual herbs that respond rapidly to seasonal rainfall.
column6: Native peppercresses (Lepidium spp.), native cresses (Rorippa spp.), bittercresses (Cardamine spp.). Many species produce pungent mustard oils that help deter herbivores and pathogens.

column3: Phyllanthaceae is a largely tropical and subtropical family of shrubs and small trees, formerly included within Euphorbiaceae. Many Australian species occur in rainforests and monsoon forests where they form part of the understory vegetation.
column6: Leaf flowers (Phyllanthus spp.), native gooseberries (Antidesma spp.). Some species have unusual flattened branch systems that resemble compound leaves.

column3: Apiaceae includes aromatic herbs with umbrella-like flower clusters called umbels. Australian native species are especially diverse in alpine, temperate and wetland habitats. Many species contain strongly scented oils.
column6: Native celery (Apium prostratum), pennyworts (Hydrocotyle spp.), sea parsley (Apium spp.). Several alpine species are adapted to frost, snow and short growing seasons.

column3: Portulacaceae contains succulent herbs often associated with rocky outcrops, dry grasslands and arid environments. Species are highly adapted to water storage and heat tolerance.
column6: Purslanes (Portulaca spp.), including native desert purslanes. Some Australian species can remain dormant for long periods and germinate rapidly after rainfall.

column3: Boraginaceae includes herbs and shrubs often recognised by rough hairy leaves and curled flower buds. Many Australian species are associated with inland and semi-arid habitats.
column6: Native forget-me-nots (Halgania spp.), bluebells (Cynoglossum spp.). Some species change flower colour as they age or after pollination.

column3: Zygophyllaceae is strongly associated with Australia’s arid interior. Many species are drought-resistant shrubs or herbs with small leaves and tough seed capsules adapted to extreme heat and low rainfall.
column6: Twin-leaf plants (Zygophyllum spp.), stinkweed (Tribulus spp.). Several species can survive prolonged drought by remaining dormant until rainfall triggers rapid growth.

column3: Rhamnaceae includes shrubs and small trees common in forests, heathlands and dry woodlands. Many Australian species have small but intricate flowers and play important roles in post-disturbance recovery.
column6: Native pomaderis (Pomaderris spp.; ~70 species), red ash (Alphitonia excelsa). Several species are among the first plants to regenerate after fire.

column3: Geraniaceae includes herbs with deeply divided leaves and distinctive beak-shaped fruits that inspired common names such as cranesbill and storksbill. Australian species are common in grasslands and open forests.
column6: Native geraniums (Geranium spp.), storksbills (Pelargonium australe). The long seed capsules can spring open explosively, flicking seeds away from the parent plant.

column3: Australian representatives are mostly tufted herbs with strappy leaves and colourful flowers adapted to dry climates and nutrient-poor soils. Many are popular in landscaping because of their hardiness.
column6: Flax lilies (Dianella spp.; ~40 Australian species). Many species produce striking blue or purple berries eaten by birds and small mammals.

column3: Combretaceae is a tropical family of trees and shrubs common in northern Australia, especially in monsoon forests, savannas and river systems. Many species are deciduous during the dry season.
column6: Damson trees (Terminalia spp.), pindan walnut (Terminalia canescens). Several species produce winged fruits adapted for wind or water dispersal.

column3: Thymelaeaceae includes shrubs and small trees often recognised by fibrous bark and strongly scented flowers. Many Australian species occur in heathlands and sandy soils.
column6: Rice flowers (Pimelea spp.; >100 Australian species). Some species are highly toxic to livestock while others are prized native ornamentals.

column3: Haloragaceae contains mostly aquatic or wetland plants associated with freshwater systems throughout Australia. Many species can grow submerged underwater while flowering above the surface.
column6: Milfoils (Myriophyllum spp.). These plants are important habitat for aquatic insects, fish and frogs.

column3: Pteridaceae is a large fern family containing species adapted to cliffs, rocky outcrops, rainforests and moist gullies. Ferns reproduce via spores rather than seeds or flowers.
column6: Maidenhair ferns (Adiantum spp.), brake ferns (Pteris spp.). Maidenhair ferns are among Australia’s most recognisable and widely cultivated native ferns.

column3: Loranthaceae contains Australia’s spectacular aerial mistletoes, which grow parasitically on tree branches while still photosynthesising. Mistletoes are ecologically important components of woodland ecosystems.
column6: Australian mistletoes (Amyema spp.; ~90 species). Their nectar-rich flowers attract honeyeaters and many birds rely on mistletoe fruits for food.

column3: Araliaceae includes rainforest trees, shrubs and climbers often with glossy leaves and umbrella-like flower clusters. Many Australian species occur in tropical and subtropical forests.
column6: Native umbrella trees (Schefflera actinophylla), ivy panax (Polyscias spp.). Large flowering clusters can attract birds, bats and insects.

column3: This family is famous for Australia’s iconic grass trees, which are strongly associated with fire-prone landscapes. Grass trees grow extremely slowly and some individuals may live for several centuries.
column6: Grass trees (Xanthorrhoea spp.; ~30 species). Their dramatic flowering spikes often appear in abundance after bushfires and can reach several metres in height.

column3: Australia’s native cypress pines belong to this ancient conifer family and are well adapted to drought, poor soils and fire-prone landscapes.
column6: Cypress pines (Callitris spp.; ~16 Australian species). Their timber is naturally termite resistant and highly valued for building and furniture making.

column3: Picrodendraceae is a mainly tropical family of shrubs and trees formerly grouped within Euphorbiaceae. Australian representatives are found mostly in northern forests and woodlands.
column6: Quinine trees (Petalostigma spp.). Some species have fruits traditionally used medicinally by Indigenous Australians.

column3: Pittosporaceae includes shrubs and trees common in rainforests, woodlands and coastal habitats. Many species have fragrant flowers and sticky seeds adapted for bird dispersal.
column5: Pittosporum angustifolium marginally; family rainforest-rich generally
column6: Cheesewoods (Pittosporum spp.; ~50 Australian species). Sticky orange seeds are often carried by birds after being eaten or attached to feathers.

column3: Celastraceae includes shrubs, climbers and small trees found in forests, rainforests and semi-arid regions. Several Australian species produce brightly coloured fruits attractive to birds.
column6: Native bittersweets (Maytenus spp.), climbing staff vines (Celastrus australis). Some species have striking orange or red seed capsules that split open when mature.

column3: Australia is one of the world’s major centres of diversity for carnivorous plants in the Droseraceae family. These remarkable plants are adapted to nutrient-poor soils and capture insects using sticky glandular leaves. Many species occur in sandy heathlands and seasonally wet habitats.
column6: Sundews (Drosera spp.; >180 Australian species). Some species can rapidly curl their leaves around trapped insects to improve digestion and nutrient uptake.

column3: Restionaceae contains rush-like plants strongly associated with nutrient-poor sandy soils and heathlands, particularly in southwestern Australia. Many species resemble sedges or rushes but belong to a distinct evolutionary lineage.
column6: Restios (Lepyrodia spp., Baloskion spp.). Many species are wind pollinated and form dense understorey vegetation in coastal heathlands.

column3: Plantaginaceae includes herbs and shrubs found in wetlands, grasslands and alpine habitats. Modern DNA studies greatly expanded the family to include many former snapdragon relatives.
column6: Native plantains (Plantago spp.), speedwells (Veronica spp.). Some alpine species are adapted to snow cover and short growing seasons.

column3: Nyctaginaceae includes herbs, climbers and shrubs often associated with arid and tropical regions. Flowers are frequently colourful and open during cooler evening conditions.
column6: Tarvines (Boerhavia spp.), spiderlings (Commicarpus spp.). Some species produce sticky fruits that attach to animals for dispersal.

column3: Ranunculaceae includes herbs often associated with cool temperate forests, alpine meadows and wet habitats. Many species have delicate flowers and finely divided leaves.
column6: Buttercups (Ranunculus spp.), native clematis (Clematis aristata). Several alpine species flower rapidly during short snow-free periods.

column3: Oxalidaceae includes herbs and small shrubs often recognised by clover-like leaves and sour-tasting tissues rich in oxalic acid. Australian species occur in forests, grasslands and rocky habitats.
column6: Native sorrels (Oxalis spp.). Many species fold their leaves in response to light or touch.

column3: Orchidaceae is one of the world’s largest flowering plant families and includes many highly specialised pollination strategies. Australia has a rich diversity of terrestrial orchids, particularly in southern heathlands and forests. Many species rely on symbiotic fungi to germinate and survive.
column6: Donkey orchids (Diuris spp.), spider orchids (Caladenia spp.), sun orchids (Thelymitra spp.). Some Australian orchids mimic female insects in shape and scent to attract pollinators.

column3: Caryophyllaceae includes herbs often with opposite leaves and swollen stem nodes. Australian species are especially common in alpine, coastal and arid habitats.
column6: Stitchworts (Stellaria spp.), pearlworts (Sagina spp.). Many species produce small but intricate star-shaped flowers.

column3: Polygonaceae includes herbs and shrubs often recognised by swollen stem joints and papery sheaths around the nodes. Australian native species occur in wetlands, saltmarshes and arid regions.
column6: Native docks (Rumex spp.), native knotweeds (Persicaria spp.). Some species are highly tolerant of flooding and saline conditions.

column3: Campanulaceae includes herbs with bell-shaped or tubular flowers often adapted for insect pollination. Australia contains many small colourful species in alpine and heathland ecosystems.
column6: Bluebells (Wahlenbergia spp.; ~40 Australian species), lobelias (Lobelia spp.). Bluebells are among Australia’s most widespread native wildflowers.

column3: Zamiaceae contains Australia’s native cycads, an ancient group of seed plants that existed alongside dinosaurs. Cycads are slow-growing and highly long-lived.
column5: Cycas spp. can occur in monsoon rainforest margins
column6: Burrawangs (Macrozamia spp.; ~40 species), Queensland nut cycads (Lepidozamia spp.). Many cycads are toxic unless carefully processed and some species can live for centuries.

column3: Anacardiaceae includes rainforest and woodland trees often producing resinous sap and fleshy fruits. Several Australian species are important canopy trees in tropical forests.
column6: Native mangoes (Buchanania spp.), tar trees (Semecarpus australiensis). Some species contain skin-irritating compounds related to those found in poison ivy.

column3: Commelinaceae includes soft-stemmed herbs often associated with moist habitats and rainforest margins. Flowers are frequently bright blue or purple but short lived.
column6: Native wandering trads (Commelina spp.), basket grasses (Cyanotis spp.). Many flowers open for only a single day.

column3: Stylidiaceae is famous for Australia’s remarkable triggerplants, which possess touch-sensitive floral columns that rapidly flick onto visiting insects to transfer pollen. The family is especially diverse in southwestern Australia.
column6: Triggerplants (Stylidium spp.; >300 Australian species). Some species may also supplement nutrients through carnivorous or proto-carnivorous behaviour.

column3: Violaceae includes herbs and shrubs with delicate flowers often found in forests, grasslands and alpine habitats.
column5: Often rainforest herbs/shrubs though sparse here
column6: Native violets (Viola spp.), hybanthus shrubs (Hybanthus spp.). Several Australian violets spread vegetatively to form dense groundcovers in moist habitats.

column3: Although less diverse in Australia than in the Northern Hemisphere, Rosaceae includes shrubs and herbs occurring in forests, alpine areas and woodlands.
column6: Native raspberries (Rubus spp.), geums (Acaena spp.). Some species produce hooked fruits that cling to animal fur for dispersal.

column3: Capparaceae includes shrubs and small trees often adapted to warm dry climates. Flowers are commonly large and showy with numerous stamens.
column6: Native capers (Capparis spp.), brush capers (Apophyllum anomalum). Some species produce strongly scented night-opening flowers pollinated by moths or bats.

column3: Rushes are grass-like plants common in wetlands, saltmarshes and seasonally wet habitats. Unlike grasses, their flowers are usually small and inconspicuous.
column6: Rushes (Juncus spp.). Rushes play important roles in erosion control and wetland filtration systems.

column3: Iridaceae includes herbs with sword-like leaves and often striking flowers. Australian species are especially diverse in southern heathlands and grasslands.
column6: Native irises (Patersonia spp.), blue flags (Diplarrena moraea). Many species flower prolifically after fire or seasonal rain.

column3: Lecythidaceae is primarily a tropical rainforest family represented in Australia by several large canopy trees.
column6: Idiospermum (Idiospermum australiense), often called the idiot fruit. This ancient rainforest species is considered a “living fossil” linked to Gondwanan rainforests.

column3: Crassulaceae includes succulent herbs adapted to drought and rocky habitats through specialised water-storage tissues.
column6: Stonecrops (Crassula spp.). Many species can reproduce vegetatively from tiny stem or leaf fragments.
